exemplar.title: How does GDP per capita correlate with CO2 emissions per capita across different countries?

detailedExemplarData.title: How does GDP per capita correlate with CO2 emissions per capita across different countries?

subject.title: Mathematics Applications & Interpretation (AI)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics: Applications and Interpretation** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **real-world applications of mathematics, statistical analysis, mathematical modeling, and data interpretation**, with a strong focus on **technology use, practical problem-solving, and interpreting mathematical results in context**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | External examination (calculator permitted) | 1h 30min | 2h | SL: 40%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 2** | External examination (technology required) | 1h 30min | 2h | SL: 40%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 3** | External examination (technology required) | N/A | 1h | HL: 20% |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal assessment | 30h | 30h | 20% |

strengths: Clear introduction, methodology, data presentation, mathematical processing and conclusion that guide the reader through the exploration.
Use of headings and sectioning to demarcate key parts of the report.
Conclusion succinctly summarises the moderate correlation and directly answers the research question.
weaknesses: Some lapses in flow â e.g. research question split across lines, short paragraphs broken across pages â interrupt coherence.
Scatter plot axes lack units (USD, Mt) and labels, leading to potential ambiguity.
Repetition of the âmoderate correlationâ finding and inconsistent bibliography formatting reduce conciseness.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work shows adequate logical development and organization with mostly relevant content, but minor structural lapses, repetition and presentation inconsistencies prevent it from reaching higher bands. Overall, its coherence and relevance fit band 2 (some coherence and logical development; adequate organisation; mostly relevant with unnecessary elements).

strengths: Appropriate notation is used for Pearsonâs r, Spearmanâs râ and the regression equation in most instances.
Effective use of multiple representations â data table, scatter plot and formulae support the discussion.
Definitions of key terms (GDP per capita, COâ emissions) are clearly articulated with supporting context.
weaknesses: Typographical and notation errors (e.g. âGPD per capitaâ, âr_{sis} 0.439â) cause confusion.
Interchange of units (Mt vs t, USD) is not explained or standardised.
Regression equation is ambiguously presented (intercept given without full y=mx+b format).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Mathematical language and representations are generally appropriate, but recurring notation slips and inconsistencies prevent consistently clear communication. This aligns with band 2 (some appropriate language; some representations; mostly clear with inconsistencies).

strengths: The choice of contrasting countries and focus on climate change reflects personal interest and some independent initiative.
weaknesses: Creativity is limited â standard correlation techniques are applied without novel extensions.
Personal approach, while evident in topic selection, does not consistently drive deeper or more original analysis.
There is little evidence of outstanding initiative beyond typical IA requirements.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although the exploration arises from genuine personal curiosity and shows some independence in dataset selection, the limited creativity and reliance on standard methods place it at band 1 (some evidence of personal approach; minimal creativity; limited independent thinking).

strengths: Reflects meaningfully on clustering, outliers and dataset limitations throughout the discussion.
Connects mathematical results to understanding of economicâenvironmental relationships and suggests general improvements for future work.
weaknesses: Critical analysis remains basic; methodological validity (e.g. sampling bias, model appropriateness) is not deeply probed.
Suggestions for further research are broad rather than proposing specific variables or diagnostics.
Reflection on statistical reliability (e.g. p-values, residual analysis) is absent.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student offers meaningful reflection and clear links to understanding but lacks depth in critical analysis and detailed critique, fitting band 2 (meaningful reflection; basic critique; clear connection to understanding).

strengths: Relevance: Pearsonâs r, Spearmanâs râ and linear regression are correctly chosen and applied to the research question.
Calculations of means, correlation coefficients and regression slope are mostly correct and interpreted in context.
Interpretation of slope connects mathematical results to real-world changes in COâ per USD GDP increase.
weaknesses: Omits key mathematical rigour â no RÂ², p-value or treatment of tied ranks in Spearmanâs method.
Intermediate steps (variance sums, residuals) are not shown, reducing transparency and depth.
Minor unit inconsistencies and lack of verification of model fit limit accuracy.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The mathematical methods used are relevant and mostly correct, demonstrating a good level of course-level understanding, but the absence of more sophisticated analysis and missing procedural detail restrict performance to band 2 (some relevant mathematics at course level; partially correct application; some understanding).

strandSelections.reasoning: Selecting variables of personal interest and deciding to merge Pearson, Spearman and regression demonstrates noticeable independent thinking beyond a template approach, warranting significant but not outstanding evidenceâband 2.

Explanations of correlation meaning, interpretation of slope and discussion of limitations demonstrate good understanding in several sections, but depth tapers when addressing methodological validity.  This aligns with âgood understanding in partsââband 3.

Calculations of means, r, râ and the regression slope appear plausible; minor unit inconsistencies and lack of verification suggest âmostly correctâ rather than fully correct, hence band 3 for accuracy.

Most material contributes directly to answering the research question; word-count is sensible.  Small irrelevancies and repetition (re-stating that correlation is âmoderateâ several times, re-explaining COâ definition) reduce conciseness, so the strand fits âRelevant and mostly conciseââband 3.

The student uses appropriate notation for Pearsonâs r, Spearmanâs râ, regression equation and statistical symbols.  There are minor slips (units Mt vs t, misplaced arrows), but overall language is correct and understandable throughout, matching the âmostly appropriateâ description of band 3.

Explanations are generally clear, but inconsistencies such as interchanging âGDPâ and âGPDâ, and the ambiguous regression coefficients, introduce some confusion.  Thus communication is âmostly clear with some consistencyâ, aligned with band 2.

Pearsonâs r, Spearmanâs râ and linear regression are all within the SL syllabus and are clearly relevant to the question.  They are applied correctly for the most part, showing appropriate level and effectiveness; sophistication (e.g., multiple regression) is absent, so band 3 fits.

A data table, a labelled scatter plot and algebraic/ statistical formulae provide multiple representations of the situation, and they are used to support discussion effectively.  No sophisticated extras (e.g., residual plot), so band 3 for effective, not advanced, use.
The student reflects on clustering, outliers, and sample limitations, showing meaningfulâthough not penetratingâreflection throughout.  This aligns with band 2 for depth.

Mathematical techniques applied are standard for correlation studies; no novel modelling or inventive data manipulation is undertaken.  Some initiative (dual correlation measures) is present but limited, fitting the lower band 1 for creativity.

The student structures the IA with a clear introduction, methodology, data presentation, mathematical processing, interpretation and conclusion, so the narrative is easy to follow.  Logical links are made between sections (e.g., data â Pearson/Spearman â interpretation), although a few jumps occur when explaining formulas.  Overall coherence is evident but not polished to an âexcellentâ level, justifying band 3.

Critical analysis is basic: results are reported and briefly linked to possible âother factors,â but there is little probing of methodology validity or statistical limitations.  That limited critique places the work in band 1.

The student explains how the mathematical findings alter understanding of economic/environmental links, and suggests improvements.  The connection to understanding is clear but not deeply developed, meriting band 2.

Headings clearly demarcate introduction, methodology, mathematics and conclusion, giving the work a recognisable report structure.  Transitions are adequate, and tables/graphs are placed close to related discussion.  Minor lapses (e.g., short paragraphs split across pages) prevent a seamless flow; hence a solid but not exceptional band 3 is appropriate.

The exploration is driven by the studentâs own curiosity about climate change and wealth; the choice of 25 contrasting countries shows a personal route rather than a canned dataset.  This clear personal approach corresponds to band 2.

annotations.comment: Specify the exact year of the data used to ensure the analysis can be replicated accurately.
The simple regression description omits evaluation of fit quality (e.g., RÂ²); including this metric would deepen the mathematical analysis.
When using Spearmanâs rank, outline how tied ranks were handled, as this affects the accuracy of râ.
The table header reads âGPD per capitaâ instead of âGDP per capitaâ, which could confuse readers and needs correction.
The diversity of countries in the dataset strengthens the investigationâs representativeness and relevance.
Include summary statistics (e.g., mean, median) alongside the raw data table to support later calculations.
The scatter plot is a valuable multiple representation that visualises the relationship clearly.
The research question is concise and directly targets the relationship between GDP per capita and COâ emissions, providing clear focus for the exploration.
Splitting the research question over two lines interrupts readability; consider merging into one continuous statement for greater clarity.
The word count line adds administrative detail rather than contributing to the academic content; remove or relocate to metadata to maintain flow.
Definitions of GDP per capita and COâ emissions are clearly articulated, demonstrating effective use of mathematical language and context.
Consider citing authoritative sources for the definitions of GDP and COâ emissions to strengthen academic rigor.
Typographical error: âcontributersâ should be spelled âcontributorsâ to maintain professional presentation.
The methodology section effectively explains data collection and the rationale for country selection, showing personal engagement.
The scatter plot lacks labeled axes with units (USD, Mt); add these to avoid ambiguity in interpretation.
Consider adding a residual plot to assess the adequacy of the linear regression model and identify non-linearity.
The formula for Pearsonâs r is correctly formatted, facilitating clear mathematical communication.
Intermediate sums and variance calculations are omitted; showing these steps would increase transparency and rigor.
The absence of significance testing (e.g., p-value) for r=0.45 limits assessment of statistical reliability.
Units for COâ (Mt) and GDP (USD) are interchanged without discussion; consider standardizing units or explaining the conversion.
The regression interpretation clearly connects slope to real-world change in emissions per USD increase, demonstrating effective use of mathematics.
The intercept is presented ambiguously as âx = -120.90â; clarify the full line equation as y = 0.0165x â 120.90.
How was the appropriateness of a linear model assessed? Consider commenting on residual patterns or goodness-of-fit diagnostics.
Spearmanâs rank steps are presented clearly, with sum DÂ² and n values supporting understanding of the calculation.
Typographical error: âr_{sis} 0.439â should read âr_s = 0.439â to maintain correct notation.
Discuss the impact of tied ranks on the Spearman coefficient to demonstrate deeper critical analysis of methodology.
The discussion of cluster patterns and outliers demonstrates reflection on data structure and its implications for correlation strength.
The text repeats the moderate correlation finding without adding new insight; consider merging sentences to improve conciseness.
Link the interpretation explicitly to the value r=0.45 and râ=0.439 to reinforce coherence between numerical results and discussion.
The comparison between the United States and Singapore powerfully illustrates how similar GDPs can yield vastly different emissions, enhancing understanding.
The discussion of Sierra Leoneâs low values would benefit from contextualising population or policy factors to deepen reflection.
The conclusion succinctly summarises the key finding of moderate positive correlation and directly answers the research question.
Propose investigation of additional variables (e.g., energy policy indices) to frame specific future research directions.
The rationale for non-random sampling contradicts unbiased selection; reflect on how this choice may have introduced sampling bias.
Bibliography entries lack a consistent citation style and omit access dates for online data; standardise formatting for presentation.
Include the date of access for each online source to adhere to academic referencing conventions.
